responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity sovereignty hinges on escaping a structural dependency on Apple and Google's platform integrity APIs, app-distribution controls, and device-trust signals—a constraint that the current MitID architecture cannot remedy without wholesale replacement. The project's core tension lies between **immediate technical feasibility** (demonstrators using open standards) and **institutional adoption pressure** (securing procurement and standards language). A secondary tension pits **regulatory conservatism** (eIDAS/NSIS certification rigor, incumbent resistance) against **geopolitical urgency** (EU digital sovereignty, supplier concentration risk). The five levers below navigate these by combining coalition-building and framing work (phases 1–2) with strategic procurement intervention (phase 3), leveraging technical evidence and policy reframing to make platform-neutral requirements harder to dismiss. The levers deliberately avoid assuming constructive cooperation from operators or regulators, instead treating resistance as a design constraint that must be overcome through standards pressure, EU alignment, and credible technical proof rather than persuasion alone.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Positioning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators prove that open-standards fallback access (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, browser-based, or hardware-token alternatives) can deliver Substantial-level assurance without Apple/Google intermediation. → Systemic: Establishes a credible **architecture alternative** that reduces perceived technical risk of platform neutrality, lowering institutional barriers to procurement language by ~25–30% by shifting debate from 'is it possible?' to 'which model scales best?' → Strategic: Converts fallback access from a niche resilience feature into a **normative expectation** within EU digital identity frameworks, making exclusive platform-dependency increasingly indefensible in future procurement cycles.
options: Develop a single lean demonstrator (FIDO2/OpenID on GrapheneOS) focused narrowly on authentication-event simulation against a sandbox identity provider, with published architectural blueprints but no production integration, to establish technical feasibility with minimal scope creep and maximum credibility for regulatory review.

Build two parallel demonstrators—one mobile-oriented (GrapheneOS/WebAuthn) and one browser-based (hardware-token or push-button-based fallback)—each targeting different citizen cohorts and use cases, to provide comparative evidence for procurement decisions and position fallback access as a portfolio choice rather than a single alternative.

Prototype a hybrid mobile-plus-hardware-token stack (e.g., FIDO2 USB keys + web-based login for low-connectivity or elderly citizen access) integrated with a simulated Danish public-sector identity provider, to demonstrate that platform-neutral fallback can serve as the *primary* authentication path for defined user populations rather than as an emergency-only contingency.
levers.review_lever: Controls technical feasibility vs. institutional adoption risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the real-time operational burden of maintaining and iterating demonstrators under constrained phase-two budgets, or the likelihood that incumbent operators will dismiss prototype-stage work as 'not production-ready' regardless of architecture quality.

levers.name: Coalition Breadth and Stakeholder Lock-in
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase-one advocacy recruits civil-society, privacy-advocacy, academic, and contingency-planning stakeholders into a formal coalition, plus sympathetic Folketinget members and EU technical working groups. → Systemic: Each coalition member brings institutional legitimacy and constituency pressure; a coalition of 12–15 credible organizations increases the political cost of rejecting platform-neutrality language by ~35–40%, making procurement exclusion of fallback-access requirements more visible and contestable at cabinet and parliament level. → Strategic: Converts a single research project into a **distributed advocacy network** with multiple entry points into regulatory and standards bodies, reducing dependence on any single procurement authority and embedding platform-neutrality expectations across Danish and EU digital governance.
options: Recruit a lean coalition (5–8 lead organizations representing civil society, security research, and data-protection advocates) with quarterly alignment meetings and a single unified policy position, to maintain message discipline and fast decision-making but risk missing constituencies and reducing distributed pressure on regulators.

Build a broad, loosely federated coalition (15–20+ organizations across civil society, academia, industry, healthcare, and public administration) with working groups organized by domain (procurement, standards, resilience, privacy) and rolling consensus-building, to maximize coverage and institutional access but require significant coordination overhead and tolerate some message variance.

Establish a formal coalition with tiered membership (core 8–10 anchor organizations with voting seats, 20–30 supporting members with consultation rights) modeled on European standards-body governance, with public commitments from Folketinget digital-policy committee members and explicit EU alignment, to combine breadth with governance legitimacy but require legal/institutional setup costs.
levers.review_lever: Controls coalition reach vs. message coherence. Weakness: The options fail to account for the real incentives of member organizations (privacy NGOs may prioritize individual rights over supplier concentration; IT industry bodies may fear disruptive procurement changes; public-sector agencies may resist sovereignty language that implies institutional autonomy they do not possess).

levers.name: Procurement Language and Standardization Timing
levers.consequences: Immediate: By month 16, the project produces a concrete set of platform-neutral, fallback-access, and resilience-requirement language tailored to AltID and future Danish digital identity procurement RFPs. → Systemic: Insertion of such language into a single high-stakes procurement document (AltID or equivalent) shifts vendor design decisions upstream; suppliers face ~20–30% increased compliance engineering costs if they must architect for platform-neutral and fallback pathways, creating economic pressure to de-prioritize exclusive Apple/Google integration in competing products. → Strategic: Establishes **formal procurement precedent** that embeds platform-neutrality and fallback-access expectations into Danish public-sector digital identity baseline standards, making future deviation require explicit organizational justification and creating a **lock-in mechanism** favoring supplier diversity.
options: Propose procurement language during phase-three AltID review that mandates 'platform-neutral authentication' but leaves architectural interpretation to vendors, accepting significant variance in implementation and risking that vendors deliver token compliance while preserving de facto platform lock-in through opaque fallback mechanisms.

Co-author detailed technical procurement specifications (interoperability APIs, fallback trigger conditions, cryptographic standards, hardware-token certification paths) in consultation with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator by month 18, to increase vendor clarity and reduce implementation variance, but require intensive regulatory negotiation and risk endpoint delays to AltID timeline.

Launch a public EU-aligned digital-identity procurement standard (working title: 'Platform-Neutral Access Requirements for Substantial-Level eIDAS Wallets') through EU technical working groups and publish as a reference framework in months 20–24, to build leverage beyond Danish procurement alone and position supplier-concentration risk as a European problem requiring European-level standards intervention.
levers.review_lever: Controls Danish execution speed vs. EU-wide systemic change. Weakness: The options fail to acknowledge that procurement language insertion requires institutional champion inside Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the Ministry of Finance who faces political risk if the language causes AltID cost overruns or schedule delays, and such champions are unlikely to emerge without explicit political cover from parliament.

levers.name: Incumbent Resistance and Regulatory Capture Mitigation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase one frames the issue explicitly as supplier-concentration risk and strategic-resilience problem rather than anti-Apple/Google ideology, deflecting operator pushback that reframes platform neutrality as privacy theater or technical incompetence. → Systemic: Public feasibility report and demonstrators funded by public institutions, plus explicit coalition backing from privacy advocates and contingency-planning authorities, create **credible independent evidence** that operators cannot easily dismiss as partisan; this shifts burden of proof toward operators to explain why platform-neutral fallback cannot meet Substantial-level assurance, increasing regulatory friction to incumbent-favorable positions by ~30%. → Strategic: If operators argue that platform-neutral access violates their commercial terms or introduces unacceptable trust-model complexity, the project's documented technical evidence and standards alignment enable regulators to mandate fallback access anyway, converting operational objections into **compliance costs rather than architectural impossibilities**.
options: Engage MitID operator and Apple/Google with a neutral technical advisory board (month 4 of phase one) offering early briefing on demonstrator scope and inviting operator input on compatibility concerns, to signal good-faith collaboration and reduce claims of hostile intent, while accepting that operators may use advisory participation to learn project tactics and position defensive arguments.

Exclude operators from early-stage coalition and technical work, maintaining independent research credibility through months 1–16, and only formally engage operators in phase three when demonstrators and procurement language are already public, to preserve analytical independence and prevent premature institutional capture, but risk delayed problem-solving and stronger operator resistance during final negotiations.

Establish a formal Technical Review Board including independent cryptographic and assurance auditors appointed by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, with mandatory operator participation and published meeting minutes, to create transparent regulatory oversight and force operators to defend platform-dependency choices in a public, auditable forum by month 12.
levers.review_lever: Controls independence vs. constructive engagement. Weakness: The options fail to model what happens if an operator (particularly Apple or Google) informally signals to Danish authorities that accommodating platform-neutral access will result in service degradation, policy changes, or reduced investment in Denmark—a threat that regulators may find difficult to ignore even with independent technical evidence.

levers.name: EU Alignment and Standards Lock-in Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase three embeds the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-access findings into European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and contributes concrete language proposals to eIDAS2 implementation guidelines by month 20. → Systemic: If even 2–3 EU member states (e.g., France, Germany, Netherlands) adopt platform-neutral fallback-access language in their wallets or digital-identity RFPs by 2027, suppliers face a ~40–50% larger fragmented-compliance burden; this creates competitive advantage for vendors that pre-architect for supplier neutrality and reduces Apple/Google ability to treat Europe-wide as a consolidated negotiating block. → Strategic: Converts Danish sovereignty initiative into a **European structural standard**, making exclusive platform-dependency increasingly untenable across EU regulated services; even if Denmark's AltID procurement fails to include platform-neutrality language, EU-wide technical momentum can still force architectural change through market competition and regulatory alignment in larger member states.
options: Position Denmark as a technical contributor to European Digital Identity Wallet working groups, with explicit focus on resilience, interoperability, and reduced platform lock-in, publishing a joint technical report (months 20–24) on platform-neutral authentication pathways that becomes a reference for EU member-state digital-identity procurement guidelines.

Coordinate directly with digital-sovereignty initiatives in Germany (NIST), France (France Identité), and Netherlands (DigiD modernization) to align platform-neutrality language and fallback-access requirements across procurement documents, creating a unified EU procurement signal that suppliers cannot dismiss as niche or outlier regulation.

Propose formal amendment to eIDAS2 technical guidance (Schedule 3 or equivalent) mandating that Substantial-level wallets must include either platform-neutral authentication or a certified fallback path independent of proprietary OS integrity APIs, requiring EU-level consensus but establishing **binding legal precedent** for all member-state implementations from 2028 onward.
levers.review_lever: Controls Danish autonomous action vs. EU-wide systemic change. Weakness: The options fail to acknowledge that substantive EU-level standards amendment requires consensus or qualified-majority support from member states whose incumbent digital-identity operators (France Telecom, Deutsche Telekom, national governments) have strong incentives to preserve existing platform dependencies and may actively block harmonization language that threatens their commercial arrangements.

levers.name: Institutional Risk and Phase-Gating Discipline
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase-one deliverables (feasibility report, formal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen response, coalition matrix) are unconditionally funded and publicly published regardless of institutional reception; phase-two funding is conditional on documented engagement with at least one public-sector authority and a credible financing roadmap; phase-three scope is explicitly flexible, de-scoping to publication and EU engagement if Danish AltID procurement traction is weak by month 16. → Systemic: This gating structure forces continuous reassessment of institutional receptivity; if by month 12 Digitaliseringsstyrelsen signals that platform-neutral requirements are politically infeasible, the project pivots to EU-facing work, reducing sunk-cost risk and misallocated spend by ~35% compared to an unconstrained 24-month roadmap. → Strategic: Protects public investment and institutional credibility by decoupling technical and coalition work (phase 1–2, defensible regardless of outcome) from aggressive procurement intervention (phase 3, only pursued if grounds-truth signals support), ensuring that the project's primary outputs (feasibility evidence, demonstrators, policy reframing) deliver value even if direct Danish procurement influence fails.
options: Implement strict phase-gating with kill-switch logic: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen does not issue a formal written response to phase-one proposal by month 10, or if a feasibility report does not meet academic/technical publication standards by month 8, immediately de-scope phase two and reallocate remaining budget to EU-level standards work and coalition maintenance.

Use rolling phase-funding with quarterly reassessment: fund phase one in full, gate phase two on evidence of institutional engagement (meeting with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, published security audit of demonstrator concepts, or news coverage demonstrating public legitimacy), and gate phase three on either confirmed AltID traction OR demonstrated EU-level technical-working-group participation, allowing flexibility but requiring explicit decision-making at each gate.

Establish an independent Institutional Steering Committee (Danish academic, EU official, external assurance auditor, and civil-society representative) that meets quarterly, reviews phase progress against institutional-receptivity signals, and has authority to recommend phase-three de-scoping if Danish procurement appears closed, adding oversight layer but introducing external coordination overhead.
levers.review_lever: Controls adaptive execution vs. commitment to planned scope. Weakness: The options fail to account for the political incentive of a project coordinator or sponsoring institution to rationalize weak institutional signals ('they're just busy,' 'they'll engage in phase two') rather than triggering honest de-scoping conversations that might terminate future funding tranches.

levers.name: Technical Credibility and Security Audit Independence
levers.consequences: Immediate: The demonstrators (phase two) undergo peer review by an independent external cryptographer and mobile-security auditor appointed by the project's Institutional Steering Committee, with published audit findings released by month 18 regardless of whether vulnerabilities are found. → Systemic: Publication of an external audit (even one identifying design limitations or assumptions) increases institutional credibility by ~40% compared to self-tested work; regulators and operators cannot easily dismiss findings as marketing or advocacy, and audit findings become a **durable reference artifact** in procurement and standards discussions. → Strategic: Establishes the project's technical work as **independent scholarship** rather than vendor positioning, creating lasting intellectual authority that persists beyond the 24-month project lifecycle and can be cited in future regulatory and standards debates.
options: Commission a single comprehensive security audit of both demonstrators (mobile and fallback) by a single external firm (e.g., NCC Group, Trail of Bits, or equivalent Danish-based security consultancy) by month 18, with findings published publicly, to achieve clear accountability and unified audit narrative but risk bottleneck if auditor capacity is constrained.

Engage a panel of three independent auditors (one cryptographic specialist, one mobile-OS security researcher, one hardware-token/WebAuthn expert), each reviewing their respective domain component in parallel during months 15–18, to distribute risk and combine specialized expertise, but require coordination overhead and tolerance for some finding variance across auditors.

Establish a rolling audit process with quarterly security reviews by rotating external assessors during phases one and two, and a final comprehensive third-party audit by month 19, to surface vulnerabilities iteratively and allow mid-course architectural corrections, but distribute budget across multiple auditors and lengthen total review timeline.
levers.review_lever: Controls credibility and rigor vs. execution timeline and budget. Weakness: The options fail to address the fact that any external auditor's willingness to publish critical findings depends on contractual independence and professional reputation incentives; if the audit is commissioned by the project and the auditor fears reputational damage from 'killing' a publicly-funded initiative, they may soften findings or hedge conclusions in ways that reduce regulatory impact.

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-positioned to navigate Denmark's digital-identity sovereignty challenge by combining technical demonstration, coalition-building, and procurement/standards intervention—each lever targets a distinct institutional or technical barrier. However, they underweight one critical dimension: **real-time engagement with incumbent operators' technical and commercial constraints**. While Lever 4 addresses resistance mitigation, it does not provide concrete pathways for demonstrators to integrate with live MitID or AltID testing environments; if the project's fallback-access proofs remain purely sandbox-based, operators can credibly claim that real-world deployment introduces unquantified trust-model or interoperability risks that the demonstrations did not surface. **Recommendation: Add to Lever 1 ('Fallback Authentication Pathway Positioning') a third option that seeks limited, read-only technical access to MitID's sandbox environment or AltID's test certification infrastructure (months 9–14 of phase two), allowing demonstrators to validate compatibility assumptions and cross-check assurance claims against actual Danish identity provider behavior, rather than relying wholly on simulator-based proof. Frame this as a 'compatibility validation' rather than production integration, to preserve independence while reducing operators' ability to claim unknown real-world risks.**

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a structural digital-sovereignty crisis: MitID's dependency on Apple/Google platform signals creates single-point-of-failure risk for essential public services. The project's core tension is between legitimacy (requiring technical credibility and institutional buy-in from conservative stakeholders) and speed (needing visible progress before regulatory windows close). The chosen levers navigate this by decoupling technical demonstration from deployment, framing resilience as a procurement requirement rather than ideology, and building multi-stakeholder coalitions that reduce incumbent vendor veto power. Success hinges on converting feasibility evidence and standards pressure into procurement language before AltID hardens existing dependencies.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope and Trust-Model Realism
levers.consequences: Immediate: Narrowing demonstrator focus to FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS reduces implementation cost and complexity by ~40% vs. full MitID integration. → Systemic: Credible non-production proof-of-concept increases institutional credibility with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by shifting conversation from 'is it theoretically possible' to 'we have working evidence'; reduces incumbent vendor dismissal risk by ~30% because demonstrator is transparently limited and not positioned as MitID replacement. → Strategic: Lean, defensible demonstrators preserve coalition credibility and allow phase-three procurement leverage without overextending technical team, but risk being dismissed as insufficiently production-ready if not carefully positioned as deliberate architectural research rather than product development.
options: Build two parallel demonstrators (mobile + browser/hardware-token fallback) with explicit non-production status and detailed architecture documentation showing how each could scale to Substantial eIDAS2 level without platform vendor dependencies.

Deliver a single unified demonstrator integrating FIDO2, OpenID Connect, and simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider APIs, prioritizing internal coherence over breadth, and position it as a reference implementation for vendor procurement teams.

Commission an independent external research institution to build and publish a demonstrator in parallel with project work, creating operational distance from project funding and reducing appearance of advocacy-driven engineering.
levers.review_lever: Controls feasibility credibility vs. technical scope creep. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether demonstrators should include explicit fallback-to-SAML or legacy authentication pathways that existing public-sector systems could connect to without re-platforming.

levers.name: Coalition Sequencing and Veto-Player Mapping
levers.consequences: Immediate: Early engagement with Datatilsynet (Danish Data Protection Authority) and contingency-planning teams in Forsvarsstyrelsn (Defense Command) locks institutional allies into platform-neutrality framing before incumbent vendors mobilize opposition. → Systemic: Coalition spanning civil society, academia, regulators, and national-security stakeholders increases procurement influence by ~50% over single-stakeholder lobbying and creates distributed advocacy that MitID operator cannot dismiss as fringe; documented veto-player map identifies which actors (e.g., AltID board members, EU Working Group participants) can block or accelerate platform-neutrality requirements. → Strategic: Early coalition visibility may provoke preemptive incumbent pushback, but creates defensive advantage: if vendors lobby against platform-neutrality requirements, public record of coalition endorsement frames resistance as self-interested rather than technically justified.
options: Map and sequentially engage veto players starting with national-security and contingency-planning stakeholders (whose resilience mandate is hardest to dismiss), then add regulatory allies (Datatilsynet, Rigsrevisionen), then civil-society and academic voices, using each tier's credibility to validate next tier's legitimacy.

Initiate simultaneous engagement across all stakeholder clusters (regulators, operators, vendors, civil society, EU bodies) in month 1 to create visibility and deter coordinated incumbent opposition, accepting risk of premature vendor mobilization in exchange for distributed influence.

Restrict phase-one coalition building to publicly named academic and civil-society partners while conducting confidential regulatory and national-security briefings, delaying broader visibility until demonstrators and feasibility report create technical anchor for institutional commitments.
levers.review_lever: Controls transparency vs. stealth. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether coalition members should sign a shared non-disclosure or commitment agreement that locks them into public endorsement of platform-neutrality language, reducing free-riding and vendor pressure to publicly distance themselves from the project.

levers.name: Standards Positioning and EU Wallet Alignment
levers.consequences: Immediate: Formal engagement with EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and eIDAS2 implementation bodies establishes the project as expert input on fallback-authentication and interoperability requirements. → Systemic: Documented input to EU standards processes creates cascade pressure on Danish AltID: if European Wallet framework includes platform-neutral or fallback-access language, Danish procurement teams face coordination pressure to align, reducing excuse for vendor lock-in by ~25% and shifting burden of proof from platform-neutrality advocates to incumbent vendors claiming incompatibility. → Strategic: EU-level alignment multiplies project influence but creates timing risk: if European Wallet finalizes before project produces credible technical input, Danish AltID will follow European defaults (which may or may not be vendor-neutral); conversely, early EU engagement without technical evidence is dismissed as premature lobbying.
options: Participate directly in ETSI working groups and European Commission eIDAS2 implementation task forces starting month 3, positioning project feasibility report and demonstrators as technical input to EU Wallet interoperability requirements, with explicit goal of including platform-neutral authentication paths in official EU guidance.

Publish project findings and demonstrator documentation as peer-reviewed security and digital-identity conference papers, allowing EU standards bodies and vendors to discover and reference the work independently, reducing appearance of advocacy and increasing credibility with technical committees.

Negotiate a formal research partnership with a prominent EU digital-identity research consortium (e.g., OpenIDEO or equivalent) to jointly develop fallback-authentication specifications that EU Wallet bodies can adopt, creating co-authored standards authority rather than single-nation input.
levers.review_lever: Controls narrative ownership vs. distributed influence. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the project should explicitly propose a baseline EU platform-neutrality requirement (e.g., 'all EU Wallet implementations must include a non-proprietary fallback authentication path') or frame platform-neutrality as optional interoperability feature, risking weak institutional adoption if left optional.

levers.name: Regulatory Framing and Resilience Narrative Ownership
levers.consequences: Immediate: Reframing MitID/AltID platform dependency as a resilience, contingency-planning, and supplier-concentration risk (rather than privacy or ideology) shifts conversation from 'do we want to reduce platform control' to 'do we want critical public services depending on two foreign vendors whose policies, geopolitical exposure, or app-store practices could change unilaterally'. → Systemic: Formal risk language in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and Rigsrevisionen documentation increases political cost of ignoring platform-neutrality requirements by ~35%, because resilience and contingency planning are established regulatory priorities; incumbent vendors cannot easily dismiss resilience as fringe concern. → Strategic: Resilience framing is harder for vendors to attack publicly (they cannot argue 'no, Denmark should remain dependent on us'), but requires sustained messaging discipline and documented risk scenarios; if coalition loses framing battle and press focuses on cost or inconvenience instead of resilience, the entire leverage advantage collapses.
options: Commission Rigsrevisionen (Danish National Audit Office) to conduct a formal audit of digital-identity supplier concentration risk and contingency-planning exposure, positioning the audit as independent institutional assessment rather than project advocacy, ensuring regulatory authority and high political salience.

Develop a detailed contingency-planning scenario document (e.g., 'Apple App Store removes MitID for three months due to geopolitical sanctions') showing concrete impact on citizen access to healthcare, tax, banking, and welfare services, then circulate through Forsvarsstyrelsn and contingency-planning networks to lock resilience framing into national-security planning language.

Embed resilience and platform-neutrality language directly into AltID procurement briefs and design consultation documents through direct engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen project team, treating regulatory requirement as negotiable design parameter rather than external advocacy.
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory legitimacy vs. project neutrality. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the resilience narrative should explicitly name geopolitical scenarios (e.g., US sanctions, Apple removal of services from EU) or remain domestic-focused (e.g., vendor policy changes), risking either dismissal as scaremongering or loss of urgency if scenarios feel remote.

levers.name: Budget Sequencing and Phase-Conditional Gating
levers.consequences: Immediate: Releasing phase-one funding unconditionally (3.0 million DKK) while conditioning phase-two and phase-three funding on documented institutional engagement ensures that feasibility research and coalition building proceed even if political momentum stalls. → Systemic: Phase-conditional gating reduces financial risk by ~40% (if phase two demonstrates weak institutional traction by month 14, phase three can pivot to publication and EU standards work rather than pursuing expensive AltID procurement intervention); but increases complexity of milestone verification and creates risk that phase-two funding denial is perceived as political interference. → Strategic: Explicit funding discipline increases stakeholder confidence that project is serious about deliverables, but creates reputational risk if phase two or three funding is withheld because institutional conditions are unmet; requires transparent phase-gating criteria published before project launch to avoid accusation of arbitrary defunding.
options: Establish formal independent review board (academic, regulatory, civil-society representatives) that evaluates phase-conditional gating criteria in month 8 and month 16, publishing detailed written rationale for funding approval or deferral, ensuring transparent and defensible go/no-go decisions.

Structure funding so that phase-two and phase-three work proceeds at reduced scope if conditional triggers are not fully met, rather than full defunding, allowing project continuity and limiting reputational damage while maintaining financial discipline.

Negotiate pre-approval from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority for phase-two and phase-three funding contingent on basic feasibility-report publication and one documented stakeholder engagement, removing uncertainty and reducing risk that political changes or incumbent vendor pressure cause mid-project defunding.
levers.review_lever: Controls financial discipline vs. project continuity risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether phase-two funding should be conditioned on independent external security review of demonstrators (adding cost and timeline risk) or whether project-internal review plus peer feedback suffices to validate technical credibility.

levers.name: Incumbent Vendor Engagement and Neutrality Positioning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Offering Apple and Google formal non-threatening briefings on project goals and demonstrator architecture positions the work as technical research rather than hostile disruption, reducing preemptive vendor opposition and creating possibility of constructive vendor feedback. → Systemic: Documented vendor engagement (meeting minutes, written responses) creates paper trail showing project acted in good faith; if vendors refuse engagement or lobby publicly against platform-neutrality requirements, project can reference refusal in regulatory submissions, shifting narrative from 'outside critics attacking platform vendors' to 'vendors refusing to engage with resilience concerns'. → Strategic: Formal vendor engagement risks telegraphing project strategy and allowing vendors to mobilize counter-advocacy, but refusal to engage vendors appears secretive and invites accusations of bad-faith attack; requires careful timing and message discipline to avoid appearing either obsequious or hostile.
options: Invite Apple and Google security and policy leadership to confidential technical briefings (month 3–4) presenting demonstrator architecture and feasibility findings, explicitly framing the conversation as 'we are exploring resilience requirements, not attacking your business models,' and requesting constructive feedback on fallback-authentication specifications.

Publish a formal open letter to Apple and Google (month 6) requesting written positions on whether they support platform-neutral fallback authentication paths for critical government services, positioning their responses as public commitment to resilience regardless of vendor answer, creating record for future regulatory discussions.

Exclude Apple and Google from direct engagement until phase-three procurement discussions are underway, allowing project to establish technical credibility and coalition consensus first, then engage vendors only when leverage is greatest and they cannot redirect the conversation toward early termination or de-prioritization.
levers.review_lever: Controls transparency vs. strategic advantage. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether vendor engagement should include explicit requests for technical collaboration (e.g., Apple/Google helping specify fallback-authentication APIs) or only informational briefings, risking either co-optation or dismissal depending on vendor receptiveness.

levers.name: Publication Strategy and Knowledge Credibility
levers.consequences: Immediate: Publishing feasibility report and demonstrator documentation through combination of peer-reviewed academic venues, open-source repositories, and policy briefs ensures that project findings are available to international researchers, EU standards bodies, and Danish procurement teams regardless of political pressure or incumbent vendor objections. → Systemic: Multi-channel publication (academic, policy, open-source) increases project credibility and durability by ~50% compared to single report; demonstrates that work is scientifically rigorous rather than advocacy-driven, and creates distributed knowledge base that cannot be suppressed by single unfavorable institution or vendor pressure. → Strategic: Publishing academic and policy papers simultaneously increases complexity and requires careful sequencing (peer review timelines often exceed project phases), but creates persistent record that resilience and platform-neutrality are legitimate technical research questions; early publication also risks revealing strategic intent before AltID procurement window, giving vendors time to prepare counter-messaging.
options: Publish feasibility report as formal policy brief through Danish Research Foundation or independent research institute (month 8), simultaneously submit demonstrator architecture and fallback-authentication specifications to IEEE Security & Privacy and USENIX Security peer-reviewed conferences (targeting month 12–14 publication), and release all code and documentation on open-source platforms under GPLv3 or equivalent.

Embargo all detailed technical and policy publications until month 20 (after AltID design consultation period), releasing only a high-level summary in months 8–12 to maintain surprise advantage and reduce vendor time to counter-advocate, then publish full feasibility report, papers, and code in final project month to create lasting knowledge record.

Establish a rolling publication pipeline: release monthly technical updates and coalition briefings through project website and stakeholder email list starting month 2, maintaining visibility and momentum, then publish formal peer-reviewed papers and comprehensive reports in months 20–24 after demonstrators and institutional engagement are complete and documented.
levers.review_lever: Controls knowledge persistence vs. strategic timing. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether publications should include explicit disclaimers stating that findings are independent research and not endorsements by government partners, or whether government co-authorship should be sought to increase institutional credibility at risk of reducing independent voice.

responses.summary: These seven levers are well-distributed across technical, institutional, and strategic dimensions. However, a critical missing element is **financial and vendor counteroffensive resilience**: the levers assume that budget and political will remain stable, but do not account for incumbent vendors mobilizing legal, technical, or financial countermeasures (e.g., patent disputes, claims that fallback authentication violates platform terms, or public statements portraying platform-neutrality as inferior security). Add to **Incumbent Vendor Engagement and Neutrality Positioning** a fourth option: 'Develop contingency legal and technical defense briefs documenting that platform-neutral fallback authentication complies with EU regulation and does not infringe Apple/Google IP, enabling rapid response to vendor cease-and-desist or anti-competitive accusations.' This hardens the coalition against predictable incumbent attacks and prevents legal FUD from derailing phase-two or phase-three work.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity infrastructure faces a critical dependency on Apple and Google as implicit trust anchors—not merely as distribution channels, but as mediators of device integrity, app-authenticity signals, and platform-level telemetry essential to MitID's anti-fraud model. The core tension is between the urgent need to reduce supplier concentration and strategic autonomy before the next identity stack (AltID/eIDAS2) hardens the same lock-in, and the institutional inertia, regulatory complexity, and incumbent resistance that make direct displacement impractical. The selected levers navigate this by combining credible technical demonstration, procurement-language intervention, standards alignment, coalition sequencing, and regulatory narrative reframing. Rather than attempting to disrupt MitID directly, the project leverages the AltID transition window and EU-level momentum to shift the design assumptions and procurement rules governing the next generation of Danish digital identity—making platform neutrality and fallback resilience 'business-as-usual' rather than optional.

levers.name: Demonstrator Validation and Threat-Model Realism
levers.consequences: Immediate: Building two non-production demonstrators (mobile-oriented using GrapheneOS/FIDO2 and browser-based/hardware-token fallback) produces tangible proof-of-concept evidence that platform-neutral authentication is technically feasible without sacrificing security assurance. Systemic: Each demonstrator reduces incumbent counter-argument from 'impossible' to 'requires architectural trade-offs'; external security audits of demonstrators lower the perception of risk by 15–25% among procurement decision-makers, shifting the burden of proof to operators defending exclusive platform dependency. Strategic: Demonstrators enable the project to move from pure advocacy into concrete technical credibility, making platform-neutral requirements enforceable in procurement language rather than dismissed as theoretical.
options: Develop a minimal GrapheneOS/FIDO2 mobile demonstrator with external pen-test audit and a separate low-complexity browser-based fallback (hardware token or OTP over SMS), each tested against simulated public-sector IdP but not certified as MitID-compatible, and publish results as open technical reference.

Build a single unified mobile demonstrator that bridges FIDO2, OpenID Connect, and push-notification alternatives using industry-standard protocols, deliberately avoiding any MitID connection or certification pathway, and conduct internal security review only to maintain lean budget.

Construct a fully-feature-parity prototype spanning mobile, web, and hardware authentication that mimics MitID's anti-fraud logic and user experience, secure additional contracting budget for third-party certification exploration, and prepare a formal technical transition roadmap for institutional adoption.
levers.review_lever: Controls the tension between demonstrator simplicity (cost, schedule, defensibility) vs. demonstrator credibility (scope, security rigor, procurement relevance). Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk that demonstrators will be dismissed as 'not production-ready' by incumbent operators unless external validation comes from a widely-trusted third party (e.g., a major EU security lab or national cybersecurity agency), which is not budgeted and may not be secured.

levers.name: Coalition Assembly and Veto-Player Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Identifying and sequencing engagement with civil-society organisations, academic researchers, security experts, sympathetic Folketinget members, contingency-planning stakeholders, and privacy advocates creates a coalition that can amplify policy proposals and counterbalance incumbent operator resistance. Systemic: A coalition spanning 8–12 credible institutions reduces the political cost to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen of adopting supplier-concentration and resilience language by 30–40%, because platform-neutral requirements can be framed as a consensus position rather than a single project's advocacy; veto players (e.g., MitID operator, telco associations) become isolated if they object to widely-endorsed proposals. Strategic: Coalition sequencing determines whether phase-three procurement intervention succeeds or stalls; early engagement with sympathetic Folketinget members can insert fallback-access language into budget motions, establishing political expectation before formal AltID procurement begins.
options: Map and engage veto players first (MitID operator, largest telcos, incumbent identity brokers), position the project as resilience-enhancing rather than disruptive, and invite them into co-design discussions on fallback mechanisms, accepting dilution of demands in exchange for reduced active opposition.

Build coalition outward from academic institutions, civil-society organisations, and security research networks, deliberately excluding incumbent operators until phase two, then present demonstrators and coalition consensus as leverage to force negotiated inclusion rather than seeking early buy-in.

Assemble a parallel coalition of international digital-sovereignty and EU-level identity advocates (e.g., organisations active in eIDAS2 discussions, Estonian/EU wallet working groups), anchor Danish proposal in EU momentum and comparative benchmarking, and use European peer pressure as leverage with Danish incumbents and regulators.
levers.review_lever: Controls the trade-off between early incumbent buy-in (reducing opposition but diluting platform-neutrality demands) vs. external coalition credibility (maximising leverage but risking incumbent mobilisation against the project). Weakness: The options fail to consider the political cost if key Folketinget members or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen officials perceive the project as an end-run around established stakeholder consultation processes, which could trigger regulatory backlash or budget deferral rather than receptiveness.

levers.name: Regulatory Framing and Continuity-Planning Narrative
levers.consequences: Immediate: Reframing the issue from 'privacy vs. platform dependency' to 'business continuity, supplier concentration risk, and essential-service resilience' shifts the conversation from ideological to risk-management terms that resonate with public-sector procurement and contingency planners. Systemic: Narratives emphasising geopolitical scenarios (e.g., 'What if Apple restricts app distribution in EU markets?', 'What if platform integrity APIs become unavailable?') increase the perceived likelihood of supplier disruption by 20–35% in internal risk assessments, making fallback-access requirements defensible as prudent contingency planning rather than speculative sovereignty concerns. Strategic: Continuity-planning framing makes platform-neutral access a shared responsibility of IT continuity teams, infrastructure architects, and crisis-management offices—not just digital identity specialists—broadening institutional pressure on AltID design and procurement.
options: Anchor the project's policy narrative strictly to documented supply-chain disruption scenarios (e.g., Apple App Store regional restrictions, platform API deprecations, historical mobile-OS outages), supported by case studies from other jurisdictions, and position platform-neutral requirements as baseline resilience comparable to backup power or disaster-recovery systems.

Develop a scenario-planning exercise with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and contingency-planning teams that model the cost and operational impact of losing exclusive Apple/Google app-distribution access for 48–72 hours or longer, use quantified impact estimates to justify fallback-access procurement requirements, and publish results as a risk benchmark.

Introduce a broader 'critical-infrastructure independence' narrative linking digital identity resilience to national security, energy resilience, and food-supply continuity, position platform neutrality as a component of systemic resilience, and engage Danish Defence and emergency-management agencies as stakeholder coalition members.
levers.review_lever: Controls the tension between risk-focused narrative (grounded, credible, regulatory-aligned) vs. sovereignty-focused narrative (inspirational, politically appealing, harder to operationalise). Weakness: The options fail to consider that incumbent operators and platform vendors (if consulted) will counter with statistics on platform uptime, argue that fallback mechanisms create false assurance, or reframe the risk as 'minimal and already mitigated by existing controls', requiring the project to maintain detailed counter-evidence and resist narrative dilution.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Intervention and Timing Lock-in
levers.consequences: Immediate: Inserting platform-neutrality and fallback-access language into AltID design requirements, technical specifications, or procurement documents before formal vendor selection begins (target: by month 16) locks in supplier-neutral design assumptions at the architectural level, reducing the likelihood that incumbents can justify exclusive platform dependency post-award. Systemic: Procurement documents embedding 'shall not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores', 'shall include certified fallback authentication path', or 'shall support hardware-token or browser-based access' reduce implementation flexibility for vendors by 15–20%, increasing system-integration costs for vendors seeking shortcut reliance on platform integrity APIs, and creating competitive advantage for vendors offering multi-OS or platform-neutral solutions. Strategic: Procurement lock-in in phase three determines whether the next generation of Danish digital identity inherits the current duopoly dependency or establishes a precedent for platform-neutral design that influences subsequent eIDAS2 wallet implementations across northern Europe.
options: Engage AltID procurement team by month 14 with draft procurement language embedding platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements, request formal incorporation into technical specifications (not merely 'consideration'), and establish monthly check-ins with procurement to monitor compliance through vendor-selection and design-review gates.

Position phase-two demonstrators and feasibility report as 'reference implementations' in AltID procurement documents, explicitly citing them as proof that platform-neutral access meets security and usability thresholds, and request that AltID procurement team incorporate demonstrator source code or architecture patterns as evaluation criteria for vendor proposals.

Bypass direct AltID procurement engagement and instead target the EU eIDAS2 wallet framework working groups and technical standards bodies, position Danish platform-neutrality language as contributing to 'European interoperability and resilience', feed approved EU-level language back into Danish procurement as a compliance requirement, and avoid the appearance of direct pressure on Danish operators.
levers.review_lever: Controls the trade-off between direct procurement intervention (high impact, high political friction, risk of incumbent mobilisation) vs. EU-mediated intervention (lower direct friction, slower timescale, uncertain translation into Danish procurement). Weakness: The options fail to consider that AltID may already have vendor commitments or architecture decisions locked in by month 14, or that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may view platform-neutrality language as vendor-hostile, leading to rejection of procurement-language insertion and forcing the project to rely on softer influence channels (e.g., policy advisory groups, non-binding guidance documents).

levers.name: External Security Validation and Incumbent Counter-Narrative Neutralization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Commissioning independent security audits of phase-two demonstrators by credible third parties (e.g., a major Nordic or EU security lab, national cybersecurity authority, or internationally-recognized identity-security specialists) produces objective evidence that platform-neutral authentication does not introduce unmanaged security risks. Systemic: Independent validation reduces incumbent operator counter-arguments ('fallback access creates attack surface', 'FIDO2 is unproven for critical infrastructure') from rhetorical claims to technical refutations; publication of audit reports increases trust in demonstrators among procurement decision-makers by 25–40%, particularly if audits include explicit comparison of security assumptions between platform-neutral and platform-dependent approaches. Strategic: Third-party validation shifts the burden of proof: incumbents must now provide evidence that exclusive platform dependency is inherently more secure, rather than relying on inertia and convenience arguments to dismiss platform-neutral alternatives.
options: Commission a full ISO 27035 or equivalent security audit of both demonstrators by a major Nordic security firm or Danish Defence's cybersecurity agency, with explicit scope covering threat modelling, anti-fraud resilience, and comparison to MitID security assumptions; publish audit reports in full to maximize credibility and policy impact.

Engage the EU's European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) or a comparable EU-level body to review demonstrators as contributions to eIDAS2 wallet security frameworks, secure a formal ENISA working-group recommendation endorsing platform-neutral access for critical identity, and position Danish project as pilot evidence supporting broader EU standards.

Conduct a lightweight internal security review and third-party threat-modelling workshop with Danish academic security researchers and MitID operators (if willing), frame validation as collaborative rather than adversarial, accept less formal audit conclusions to preserve stakeholder relationships and avoid perceived 'gotcha' framing.
levers.review_lever: Controls the tension between deep, independent validation (high credibility, high cost, potential for incumbent criticism of audit methodology) vs. lightweight, collaborative validation (lower cost, faster, weaker external credibility). Weakness: The options fail to consider that major security labs may decline engagement if the project is perceived as politically contentious, or that incumbent operators may dispute audit findings by claiming demonstrators are 'not representative of production identity systems', requiring the project to establish a formal advisory board of security experts to defend audit conclusions publicly.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment and Comparative Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: Mapping Danish platform-neutrality proposals onto EU eIDAS2 wallet framework requirements and aligning with emerging standards discussions in EU technical working groups positions Denmark as a contributor to European digital identity interoperability, not merely a national sovereignty advocate. Systemic: EU-level alignment increases the political cost for Danish incumbents to resist platform-neutral requirements, because obstruction appears to violate European interoperability directives; simultaneously, alignment creates competitive pressure from EU vendors and digital-identity initiatives (e.g., Estonia, Luxembourg, Italy) exploring platform-neutral approaches, potentially reducing MitID operator's ability to claim 'platform-exclusive design is technically inevitable'. Strategic: EU standards leverage transforms platform-neutrality from a bilateral Danish negotiation into a European conformance obligation, making non-compliance with fallback-access or supplier-diversity requirements a potential barrier to EU wallet integration and market access.
options: Publish Danish feasibility report and demonstrators as inputs to official EU eIDAS2 wallet working groups by month 18, explicitly frame platform-neutrality language as a 'European best practice' for interoperability and resilience, and request formal EU working-group acknowledgement of Danish contribution, positioning it as a European standard rather than Danish idiosyncrasy.

Conduct a structured comparative analysis of platform-dependency and fallback-access approaches across Estonia, Germany, Luxembourg, and other EU wallet pilots, publish findings as a 'European benchmark report' showing which jurisdictions are adopting platform-neutral or multi-OS designs, and use comparative evidence to argue that exclusive Apple/Google dependency is an outlier rather than industry standard.

Establish a formal working relationship with the European Commission's DG DIGIT (responsible for eIDAS2 implementation) or the EU's digital identity technical secretariat, brief them on Danish platform-neutrality concerns, and request that EU guidance documents and interoperability standards include language supporting certified fallback access and supplier diversification.
levers.review_lever: Controls the tension between deep EU integration (high leverage, longer timescale, dependent on EU-level coordination) vs. Danish-first framing (faster timeline, direct control, weaker external validation). Weakness: The options fail to consider that EU technical working groups may have already reached consensus on wallet architecture by month 18, leaving limited room for Danish input, or that EU guidance may remain non-binding on national procurement, reducing the practical leverage of EU standards alignment on Danish operators.

levers.name: Phase-Gating Conditions and Contingency De-Scoping
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establishing clear phase-two and phase-three funding gates tied to measurable deliverables (published feasibility report, formal institutional engagement, demonstrator completion, AltID procurement traction) ensures that budget allocation reflects actual progress and permits rapid de-scoping if institutional resistance prevents phase-three procurement intervention. Systemic: Conditional gating avoids sunk-cost commitment to phase three if by month 16 it is evident that AltID procurement is already locked or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has rejected platform-neutrality language; project pivots to publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input at 30–40% lower cost, preserving credibility rather than spending phase-three budget on failed direct procurement lobbying. Strategic: Explicit de-scoping criteria and contingency budgets signal to funders and stakeholders that the project is realistic about institutional friction and will redirect effort toward EU and standards leverage if Danish procurement intervention proves intractable, increasing funder confidence and enabling faster decision-making if conditions change.
options: Define phase-three funding gates rigidly: release 3.5 million DKK for phase three only if (1) feasibility report is published, (2) at least two formal institutional responses (from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, or AltID governance bodies) are documented, (3) demonstrators pass external security review, and (4) AltID procurement roadmap shows openness to platform-neutrality language by month 14; if gates are not met, de-scope phase three to publication and EU engagement at 1.8 million DKK.

Establish adaptive funding: allocate a 'contingency reserve' of 500,000 DKK within phase-three budget available only if procurement obstacles arise, enabling pivot to EU standards work or coalition consolidation; make phase-three base funding (3.0 million DKK) unconditional to preserve demonstrator follow-up and publication work regardless of procurement outcome.

Define soft phase gates with explicit escalation triggers: if by month 16 Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not formally engaged on platform-neutrality language, automatically escalate to Cabinet-level digital strategy coordination and EU-liaison pathways; maintain full phase-three budget allocation, accepting higher political risk in exchange for maximum persistence in procurement intervention.
levers.review_lever: Controls the trade-off between adaptive, realistic phase-gating (preserves budget efficiency and credibility if procurement fails) vs. committed, full-scope execution (maximises procurement pressure but risks wasting phase-three budget if institutional resistance is insurmountable). Weakness: The options fail to consider that explicit de-scoping criteria, once published, may be used by opponents to argue that the project lacks confidence in its own proposals, or that contingency reserve language may trigger funder scrutiny about 'inadequate planning', requiring careful communication of de-scoping as prudent risk management rather than doubt about technical or policy merit.

responses.summary: The seven levers address the core tension between technical feasibility (demonstrators, validation) and institutional tractability (coalition sequencing, procurement intervention, phase-gating). Strengths: Levers are well-distributed across technical, policy, regulatory, and governance domains; they emphasise credible evidence-building and realistic contingency planning rather than assuming constructive cooperation from incumbents. Weaknesses: (1) Coalition Assembly and Veto-Player Sequencing risks being undermined if key political figures or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen staff perceive the project as circumventing established consultation—add explicit 'stakeholder transparency and formal notification protocols' to lever 2. (2) Phase-Gating and Contingency De-Scoping may signal low confidence unless coupled with a clearly-communicated 'no-regret' framing emphasising that EU standards and publication work is a valuable outcome even if Danish procurement fails; add a communication plan to phase-three scope defining success metrics for the de-scoped pathway. (3) AltID Procurement Intervention assumes procurement decisions are not already locked by month 14, but no mechanism exists to sense-check this assumption early—add a month-10 'procurement readiness audit' with AltID governance to validate that procurement windows remain open and adapt strategy accordingly.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Positioning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators prove that open-standards fallback access (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, browser-based, or hardware-token alternatives) can deliver Substantial-level assurance without Apple/Google intermediation. → Systemic: Establishes a credible **architecture alternative** that reduces perceived technical risk of platform neutrality, lowering institutional barriers to procurement language by ~25–30% by shifting debate from 'is it possible?' to 'which model scales best?' → Strategic: Converts fallback access from a niche resilience feature into a **normative expectation** within EU digital identity frameworks, making exclusive platform-dependency increasingly indefensible in future procurement cycles.
options: Develop a single lean demonstrator (FIDO2/OpenID on GrapheneOS) focused narrowly on authentication-event simulation against a sandbox identity provider, with published architectural blueprints but no production integration, to establish technical feasibility with minimal scope creep and maximum credibility for regulatory review.

Build two parallel demonstrators—one mobile-oriented (GrapheneOS/WebAuthn) and one browser-based (hardware-token or push-button-based fallback)—each targeting different citizen cohorts and use cases, to provide comparative evidence for procurement decisions and position fallback access as a portfolio choice rather than a single alternative.

Prototype a hybrid mobile-plus-hardware-token stack (e.g., FIDO2 USB keys + web-based login for low-connectivity or elderly citizen access) integrated with a simulated Danish public-sector identity provider, to demonstrate that platform-neutral fallback can serve as the *primary* authentication path for defined user populations rather than as an emergency-only contingency.
levers.review: Controls technical feasibility vs. institutional adoption risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the real-time operational burden of maintaining and iterating demonstrators under constrained phase-two budgets, or the likelihood that incumbent operators will dismiss prototype-stage work as 'not production-ready' regardless of architecture quality.

levers.name: Coalition Breadth and Stakeholder Lock-in
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase-one advocacy recruits civil-society, privacy-advocacy, academic, and contingency-planning stakeholders into a formal coalition, plus sympathetic Folketinget members and EU technical working groups. → Systemic: Each coalition member brings institutional legitimacy and constituency pressure; a coalition of 12–15 credible organizations increases the political cost of rejecting platform-neutrality language by ~35–40%, making procurement exclusion of fallback-access requirements more visible and contestable at cabinet and parliament level. → Strategic: Converts a single research project into a **distributed advocacy network** with multiple entry points into regulatory and standards bodies, reducing dependence on any single procurement authority and embedding platform-neutrality expectations across Danish and EU digital governance.
options: Recruit a lean coalition (5–8 lead organizations representing civil society, security research, and data-protection advocates) with quarterly alignment meetings and a single unified policy position, to maintain message discipline and fast decision-making but risk missing constituencies and reducing distributed pressure on regulators.

Build a broad, loosely federated coalition (15–20+ organizations across civil society, academia, industry, healthcare, and public administration) with working groups organized by domain (procurement, standards, resilience, privacy) and rolling consensus-building, to maximize coverage and institutional access but require significant coordination overhead and tolerate some message variance.

Establish a formal coalition with tiered membership (core 8–10 anchor organizations with voting seats, 20–30 supporting members with consultation rights) modeled on European standards-body governance, with public commitments from Folketinget digital-policy committee members and explicit EU alignment, to combine breadth with governance legitimacy but require legal/institutional setup costs.
levers.review: Controls coalition reach vs. message coherence. Weakness: The options fail to account for the real incentives of member organizations (privacy NGOs may prioritize individual rights over supplier concentration; IT industry bodies may fear disruptive procurement changes; public-sector agencies may resist sovereignty language that implies institutional autonomy they do not possess).

levers.name: Procurement Language and Standardization Timing
levers.consequences: Immediate: By month 16, the project produces a concrete set of platform-neutral, fallback-access, and resilience-requirement language tailored to AltID and future Danish digital identity procurement RFPs. → Systemic: Insertion of such language into a single high-stakes procurement document (AltID or equivalent) shifts vendor design decisions upstream; suppliers face ~20–30% increased compliance engineering costs if they must architect for platform-neutral and fallback pathways, creating economic pressure to de-prioritize exclusive Apple/Google integration in competing products. → Strategic: Establishes **formal procurement precedent** that embeds platform-neutrality and fallback-access expectations into Danish public-sector digital identity baseline standards, making future deviation require explicit organizational justification and creating a **lock-in mechanism** favoring supplier diversity.
options: Propose procurement language during phase-three AltID review that mandates 'platform-neutral authentication' but leaves architectural interpretation to vendors, accepting significant variance in implementation and risking that vendors deliver token compliance while preserving de facto platform lock-in through opaque fallback mechanisms.

Co-author detailed technical procurement specifications (interoperability APIs, fallback trigger conditions, cryptographic standards, hardware-token certification paths) in consultation with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator by month 18, to increase vendor clarity and reduce implementation variance, but require intensive regulatory negotiation and risk endpoint delays to AltID timeline.

Launch a public EU-aligned digital-identity procurement standard (working title: 'Platform-Neutral Access Requirements for Substantial-Level eIDAS Wallets') through EU technical working groups and publish as a reference framework in months 20–24, to build leverage beyond Danish procurement alone and position supplier-concentration risk as a European problem requiring European-level standards intervention.
levers.review: Controls Danish execution speed vs. EU-wide systemic change. Weakness: The options fail to acknowledge that procurement language insertion requires institutional champion inside Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the Ministry of Finance who faces political risk if the language causes AltID cost overruns or schedule delays, and such champions are unlikely to emerge without explicit political cover from parliament.

levers.name: Incumbent Resistance and Regulatory Capture Mitigation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase one frames the issue explicitly as supplier-concentration risk and strategic-resilience problem rather than anti-Apple/Google ideology, deflecting operator pushback that reframes platform neutrality as privacy theater or technical incompetence. → Systemic: Public feasibility report and demonstrators funded by public institutions, plus explicit coalition backing from privacy advocates and contingency-planning authorities, create **credible independent evidence** that operators cannot easily dismiss as partisan; this shifts burden of proof toward operators to explain why platform-neutral fallback cannot meet Substantial-level assurance, increasing regulatory friction to incumbent-favorable positions by ~30%. → Strategic: If operators argue that platform-neutral access violates their commercial terms or introduces unacceptable trust-model complexity, the project's documented technical evidence and standards alignment enable regulators to mandate fallback access anyway, converting operational objections into **compliance costs rather than architectural impossibilities**.
options: Engage MitID operator and Apple/Google with a neutral technical advisory board (month 4 of phase one) offering early briefing on demonstrator scope and inviting operator input on compatibility concerns, to signal good-faith collaboration and reduce claims of hostile intent, while accepting that operators may use advisory participation to learn project tactics and position defensive arguments.

Exclude operators from early-stage coalition and technical work, maintaining independent research credibility through months 1–16, and only formally engage operators in phase three when demonstrators and procurement language are already public, to preserve analytical independence and prevent premature institutional capture, but risk delayed problem-solving and stronger operator resistance during final negotiations.

Establish a formal Technical Review Board including independent cryptographic and assurance auditors appointed by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, with mandatory operator participation and published meeting minutes, to create transparent regulatory oversight and force operators to defend platform-dependency choices in a public, auditable forum by month 12.
levers.review: Controls independence vs. constructive engagement. Weakness: The options fail to model what happens if an operator (particularly Apple or Google) informally signals to Danish authorities that accommodating platform-neutral access will result in service degradation, policy changes, or reduced investment in Denmark—a threat that regulators may find difficult to ignore even with independent technical evidence.

levers.name: EU Alignment and Standards Lock-in Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase three embeds the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-access findings into European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and contributes concrete language proposals to eIDAS2 implementation guidelines by month 20. → Systemic: If even 2–3 EU member states (e.g., France, Germany, Netherlands) adopt platform-neutral fallback-access language in their wallets or digital-identity RFPs by 2027, suppliers face a ~40–50% larger fragmented-compliance burden; this creates competitive advantage for vendors that pre-architect for supplier neutrality and reduces Apple/Google ability to treat Europe-wide as a consolidated negotiating block. → Strategic: Converts Danish sovereignty initiative into a **European structural standard**, making exclusive platform-dependency increasingly untenable across EU regulated services; even if Denmark's AltID procurement fails to include platform-neutrality language, EU-wide technical momentum can still force architectural change through market competition and regulatory alignment in larger member states.
options: Position Denmark as a technical contributor to European Digital Identity Wallet working groups, with explicit focus on resilience, interoperability, and reduced platform lock-in, publishing a joint technical report (months 20–24) on platform-neutral authentication pathways that becomes a reference for EU member-state digital-identity procurement guidelines.

Coordinate directly with digital-sovereignty initiatives in Germany (NIST), France (France Identité), and Netherlands (DigiD modernization) to align platform-neutrality language and fallback-access requirements across procurement documents, creating a unified EU procurement signal that suppliers cannot dismiss as niche or outlier regulation.

Propose formal amendment to eIDAS2 technical guidance (Schedule 3 or equivalent) mandating that Substantial-level wallets must include either platform-neutral authentication or a certified fallback path independent of proprietary OS integrity APIs, requiring EU-level consensus but establishing **binding legal precedent** for all member-state implementations from 2028 onward.
levers.review: Controls Danish autonomous action vs. EU-wide systemic change. Weakness: The options fail to acknowledge that substantive EU-level standards amendment requires consensus or qualified-majority support from member states whose incumbent digital-identity operators (France Telecom, Deutsche Telekom, national governments) have strong incentives to preserve existing platform dependencies and may actively block harmonization language that threatens their commercial arrangements.

levers.name: Institutional Risk and Phase-Gating Discipline
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase-one deliverables (feasibility report, formal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen response, coalition matrix) are unconditionally funded and publicly published regardless of institutional reception; phase-two funding is conditional on documented engagement with at least one public-sector authority and a credible financing roadmap; phase-three scope is explicitly flexible, de-scoping to publication and EU engagement if Danish AltID procurement traction is weak by month 16. → Systemic: This gating structure forces continuous reassessment of institutional receptivity; if by month 12 Digitaliseringsstyrelsen signals that platform-neutral requirements are politically infeasible, the project pivots to EU-facing work, reducing sunk-cost risk and misallocated spend by ~35% compared to an unconstrained 24-month roadmap. → Strategic: Protects public investment and institutional credibility by decoupling technical and coalition work (phase 1–2, defensible regardless of outcome) from aggressive procurement intervention (phase 3, only pursued if grounds-truth signals support), ensuring that the project's primary outputs (feasibility evidence, demonstrators, policy reframing) deliver value even if direct Danish procurement influence fails.
options: Implement strict phase-gating with kill-switch logic: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen does not issue a formal written response to phase-one proposal by month 10, or if a feasibility report does not meet academic/technical publication standards by month 8, immediately de-scope phase two and reallocate remaining budget to EU-level standards work and coalition maintenance.

Use rolling phase-funding with quarterly reassessment: fund phase one in full, gate phase two on evidence of institutional engagement (meeting with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, published security audit of demonstrator concepts, or news coverage demonstrating public legitimacy), and gate phase three on either confirmed AltID traction OR demonstrated EU-level technical-working-group participation, allowing flexibility but requiring explicit decision-making at each gate.

Establish an independent Institutional Steering Committee (Danish academic, EU official, external assurance auditor, and civil-society representative) that meets quarterly, reviews phase progress against institutional-receptivity signals, and has authority to recommend phase-three de-scoping if Danish procurement appears closed, adding oversight layer but introducing external coordination overhead.
levers.review: Controls adaptive execution vs. commitment to planned scope. Weakness: The options fail to account for the political incentive of a project coordinator or sponsoring institution to rationalize weak institutional signals ('they're just busy,' 'they'll engage in phase two') rather than triggering honest de-scoping conversations that might terminate future funding tranches.

levers.name: Technical Credibility and Security Audit Independence
levers.consequences: Immediate: The demonstrators (phase two) undergo peer review by an independent external cryptographer and mobile-security auditor appointed by the project's Institutional Steering Committee, with published audit findings released by month 18 regardless of whether vulnerabilities are found. → Systemic: Publication of an external audit (even one identifying design limitations or assumptions) increases institutional credibility by ~40% compared to self-tested work; regulators and operators cannot easily dismiss findings as marketing or advocacy, and audit findings become a **durable reference artifact** in procurement and standards discussions. → Strategic: Establishes the project's technical work as **independent scholarship** rather than vendor positioning, creating lasting intellectual authority that persists beyond the 24-month project lifecycle and can be cited in future regulatory and standards debates.
options: Commission a single comprehensive security audit of both demonstrators (mobile and fallback) by a single external firm (e.g., NCC Group, Trail of Bits, or equivalent Danish-based security consultancy) by month 18, with findings published publicly, to achieve clear accountability and unified audit narrative but risk bottleneck if auditor capacity is constrained.

Engage a panel of three independent auditors (one cryptographic specialist, one mobile-OS security researcher, one hardware-token/WebAuthn expert), each reviewing their respective domain component in parallel during months 15–18, to distribute risk and combine specialized expertise, but require coordination overhead and tolerance for some finding variance across auditors.

Establish a rolling audit process with quarterly security reviews by rotating external assessors during phases one and two, and a final comprehensive third-party audit by month 19, to surface vulnerabilities iteratively and allow mid-course architectural corrections, but distribute budget across multiple auditors and lengthen total review timeline.
levers.review: Controls credibility and rigor vs. execution timeline and budget.
